summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about Android's opportunity, explaining why he focused on Android rather than Palm. He argues Palm and Apple share an integrated hardware-software model with a clear path, while Android's separated model remains unproven. The core problem is that Android hardware vendors aren't even trying to match iPhone quality, let alone beat it in any dimension. He warns that if Android can't compete at the high end now, Apple will gradually fill the mid-range too, leaving Android perpetually chasing next year's promise.
key_points: Palm/WebOS follows Apple's integrated model where success is a matter of execution, while Android's separated hardware-software model is fundamentally unproven
Android handset makers don't need to beat the iPhone in every way — they need to match it on basics and add one compelling differentiator
Apple will incrementally fill the mid-range market just as it did with iPod, so Android can't rely on being the cheaper alternative
The high end of the market is where you gain a foothold to grow from, not the middle
Android risks being a community forever talking about next year's phones because the current ones are second-rate
money_quotes: It's unproven whether this model can even work in the long run (where by "work" I mean "produce a phone and software platform with a state-of-the-art user experience").
If no Android handset maker can make a device that's merely as good as the iPhone, then what's the point?
The high end of the market is where you can gain a foothold, a market position to grow from.

Don't screw with the software. Let Google worry about that. The whole advantage Android offers for handset makers is that they only have to worry about the hardware — but so far they can't even get that right.
I worry that's going to be the Android community — forever talking about the next year's batch of phones, because the ones available now just are second-rate.
Match — or come really close to matching — the iPhone on these criteria and then add just one thing that beats it, at least for some segment of the market.
key_insight: Android's separated hardware-software model will fail unless at least one handset maker can match iPhone build quality and add a genuine differentiator, rather than perpetually promising next year will be better.
